result:
There is a quiet hum.

It’s not the refrigerator, though that’s part of it. It’s not the distant traffic, or the server fans in a data center miles away, or the low-frequency thrum of the electrical grid itself. It’s the combination of all of them, the unnoticed symphony of a world that is always, relentlessly, *on*.

We are creatures built for signals, for spikes in the data. The car horn, the baby’s cry, the sudden thunderclap. We are designed to notice the change, not the constant. And so, the hum becomes the sound of silence. It’s the baseline from which all other noises are measured. It’s the canvas on which the day paints its sounds.

You only truly notice it when it’s gone. During a power outage, the world doesn’t just get dark; it gets *quiet* in a way that feels profoundly wrong. The sudden absence of the hum is a presence in itself. It’s a heavy, weighted blanket of stillness. In that moment, you can hear the house settle with an unfamiliar creak. You can hear the blood pulsing in your own ears. You become aware of the sheer, naked reality of the physical world without its electric ghost. The silence is deafening because it’s the sound of the system shutting down.

And we have our own internal hums, don't we? The low-grade anxieties, the persistent memories, the quiet drone of our own inner monologue that narrates our lives. It’s always there, the background radiation of our own consciousness. We layer our days on top of it—the work projects, the conversations, the errands, the entertainment—but underneath it all, the hum of *us* persists. It’s the constant awareness that we are a self, a story in progress, a collection of hopes and regrets.

Sometimes, in moments of deep meditation or profound focus or even sheer exhaustion, that internal hum also ceases. The mind, for a blessed second, goes perfectly still. And just like the power outage, it is both terrifying and beautiful. Who are you when the inner narrator shuts up? What’s left when the hum of self-awareness fades? You’re just… here. A pair of eyes in a quiet room. A set of lungs drawing a breath. A part of the larger, silent universe.

But then the power kicks back on. The refrigerator gives a little shudder and starts its familiar drone. The digital clock on the oven blinks back to life. The hum returns, and with it, a strange sense of comfort. Normalcy is restored. The world is working again.

And in your own mind, a thought flickers. *What was I just thinking about?* The inner hum starts up again, the low, familiar vibration of being. And you get on with your day. But for a moment, you noticed it. You felt the frame around the painting, the silence that gives the noise its meaning. You appreciated the quiet, constant, life-sustaining hum of just being.
